people.name: Lester McAllister
people.narrative:
Last seen on July 4, 1918, in Liberty Park, Salt Lake City. 6-year-old Lester had attended the Independence Day celebrations with his older brother and a group of neighborhood children. His brother left him with another companion, but when he returned, Lester was gone.
Another 6-year-old told a story that Lester may have slipped and drowned in one of the ponds in the park but later said he did not know what happened. The ponds were all drained and dragged several times without success.
The possibility of abduction was also raised.
Several witnesses came forward to say they saw Lester after leaving Liberty Park and looking for his way home.
Lester was slightly small for his age. He also had a stroke of paralysis that left him with limited use of his left leg and considerably affected his speech. He had a light complexion. He was dressed in kickerbockers and a soilder's hat.

Simmons was working on a land survey for the U.S. Geological Survey for the Department of the Interior. He and three coworkers were caught in a storm and he lagged behind. His companions waved to him and he waved back, however, he never arrived back at camp.

Authorities spent 45 minutes going through maze-like connecting tunnels of a different mine entrance to reach the vehicle. No body, any blood, or evidence of foul play was located in the car and the only item recovered was a transistor radio. Utah County Sheriff's Office took this investigation at the time.

people.name: Martin Andrew Smith
people.narrative:
Last seen November 27, 2017, camping in a dry wash off Hole in the Rock Rd, Escalante. His vehicle, a 1994 Honda with California plates, was located in the wash by the sheriff's office.

Garfield County Sheriff's Office Case #: 37046

people.name: Russell Ralph Hanks
people.narrative:
Last seen October 6, 2018, in the vicinity of 3594 South Pomegranate Way, St. George. He may have been walking toward the area of Christensen Park. A surveillance camera captured him walking towards the desert south west of St. George, an area he hikes frequently.

St. George Police Department Case #: 18P025195

people.name: Jose Alfredo Mendoza
people.narrative:
Last seen on May 12, 2019 (Mother's Day). while visiting his mother is Scipio; exact location unknown/not released. He left the house and his phone was turned off within 5 minutes. His red, 2005 Subaru Forester was recovered in October 2019 in the Owl Creek Draw near the Wind River Indian Reservation.

Casper (WY) Police Department Case #: 19-028814

people.name: Clayton Lee Harris
people.narrative:
Last seen in March 2020 when he left Idaho to go to Salt Lake City.

Salt Lake City Police Department Case #: 23-255885

people.name: Eztli "Ez" Alt Cortez-Trujillo
people.narrative:
Last seen April 23, 2020, on the east side of the North Ogden divide, Weber County. The vehicle, a silver Volkswagen, was found high-centered along the road. A delivery driver stopped to offer a ride. When the driver returned after their delivery, Eztil was gone.

Salt Lake City Police Department Case #: 20-71931

people.name: Edgar Alfredo Mendez-Ramirez
people.narrative:
Last seen November 17, 2020, at his residence in Salt Lake City; exact location unknown/not released. He is originally from Guatemala and is not familiar with the Salt Lake City area.

Salt Lake City Police Department Case #: 20-207828

people.name: Jason Samir Maraviaga Boblado
people.narrative:
Last seen June 10, 2021, at a placement program in West Valley City; exact location unknown/not released. He is believed to have ran away with his biological father, Iran Maradiaga Varela, to Las Vegas, Nevada or Mexico.

West Valley City Police Department Case #: 21I021809

people.name: Vern Richard "Rick" Morris
people.narrative:
Last seen June 25, 2021, at his family home in the vicinity of 780 West and 700 S, Toole. Vern is diabetic.

Tooele City Police Department Case #: 21-T05974

people.name: Dominic Sherril Walker
people.narrative:
Last seen July 26, 2021, in the Great Salt Lake area; exact location unknown/not released. He was wearing no shoes.

NCIC Case #: M908846106, M908846106

people.name: Sacramento Cardona Gutierrez
people.narrative:
Last seen in late September/early October 2021 in Provo; exact location unknown/not released.

Provo City Police Department Case #: 22PR25885

people.name: Andrew Sebastian Rosa
people.narrative:
Last seen May 27, 2022, outside Moab when his vehicle, a blue Jeep Compass with Colorado plate CRY328, had broken down, and he went into town to seek help. The vehicle is also still missing. There are several active warrants out for his arrest from Colorado where he was a resident. He also has ties to Ohio and Wyoming.

Grand County Sheriff's Office Case #: 2302700

people.name: Cornelis "Casey" Frederik Bokslag
people.narrative:
Last seen June 6, 2022, leaving his home in the vicinity of 3808 S W Temple St, South Salt Lake City, to go to work. His vehicle, a 2012 silver Suzuki SX4 without plates, was found abandoned on a remote road at the Castle Rock exit of Interstate 80, near the Wyoming border in Summit County.

South Salt Lake City Police Department Case #: LK2022-18693

people.name: Marty Carruth Justice
people.narrative:
Last seen July 8, 2022, in Uintah County; exact location unknown/not released. He had been doing work for a family member in the Deep Creek Ranches in Dry Fork/LaPoint area. He was on probation and wearing an ankle monitor at the time of his disappearance. He had expressed his desire to abscond. Authorities tracked the monitor to a road in Deep Creek where it stopped transmitting. Searches in that area involving K9s, helicopters, airplanes and various search & rescue groups did not locate him.

Uintah County Sheriff's Office

people.name: William "Bill" Stanley Thorpe
people.narrative:
Last seen September 1, 2022, about 3 p.m. disembarking the shuttle at the Bryce Point parking lot in Bryce Canyon National Park. Multiple agencies conducted multiple searches in the area without success.

Garfield County Sheriff’s Office Case #: 0043478

people.name: Kendall Nathan Holm
people.narrative:
Last seen October 19, 2022, about 6 a.m. at his residence at the Windsor Condominiums on Quail Ridge Drive, Washington City. Kendall departed on foot without his brand new iPhone, his driver’s license, debit card or cash. He is an experienced camper and survivalist. He was an EMT and firefighter in Alaska. Kendall also liked to walk on the dirt roads on the Utah-Arizona border, but he would usually tell his father he was going there.

Washington City Police Department Case #: 22Z008265

people.name: Raymond Linn Shimer
people.narrative:
Last seen in May 2023. His daughter reported him missing. She believed he was camping in the Lehi area (exact location unknown/not released) and was concerned he was suicidal. Transient camps in the area were checked without locating Shimer.

Lehi Police Department Case #: 23LE08594

people.name: Gilberto Rubio
people.narrative:
Lat seen June 15, 2023, leaving a family/friends' residence in Orem; exact location unknown/not released. He is from California and came to Utah to visit for a few days. He stayed at a hotel in Springville and then visited relatives/friends in Orem. He was unfit to operate a motor vehicle and left the residence on foot.

Orem Police Department Case #: 23R17108
Springville Police Department Case #: 23SV04701

people.name: Justin Wayne Coursey
people.narrative:
Last seen in December 2023 in Kentucky, however his vehicle, a 2015 gray Jeep Compass, was found abandoned about 11 miles from a paved road in the Ashley National Forest, Duchesne County, Utah; location on map is approximate.

McCracken County Sheriff's Department Case #: 23-40904

people.name: Jordan Lee Gonzales
people.narrative:
Last seen March 12, 2024 in Salt Lake City; exact location unknown/not released. According to police, Jordan is considered at-risk due to living with a traumatic brain injury and is partially visually impaired. He may use the nickname JG0.

Salt Lake City Police Department Case #: 24-83208

people.name: David Allan Roe
people.narrative:
Last seen July 15, 2024, in South Salt Lake City giving out items to the homeless community; exact location unknown/not released. His vehicle, a 2001 Maroon Dodge Caravan, was found with a flat tire near the Hardware Ranch state elk preserve in Blacksmith Fork Canyon, east of Hyrum.

Orem Police Department Case #: 24-C 6481

people.name: John Edmund Kurip
people.narrative:
Last seen August 11, 2024, at his apartment located in the vicinit of 7800 S 700 E, Sandy. He called his mother to tell her he was having a mental health crisis. He left behind his wallet and cell phone. John is Native American.

Sandy Police Department Case #: SY2024-39254

people.name: Onni llmari Raassina
people.narrative:
Last seen March 5, 2025, being dropped off at the Moon Lake Lodge, near Mountain Home, Duchesne County. Onni is a Finnish national and flew to Salt Lake City airport. He then took a yellow taxi from there straight to the closed resort. It was stated he may be hiking and looking treasure in the area but was ill-equipped for the weather in the Uintas at that time of year.

Duchesne County Sheriff's Office Case #: M25-0532

people.name: Austin Bunner
people.narrative:
Last seen April 21, 2025, in Springville; exact location unknown/not released. Austin may be experiencing homelessness and may be in the Salt Lake City area.

Springville Police Department Case #: 25SV02546

people.name: Billy Headley
people.narrative:
Last seen October 12, 2025 when looking for work in Utah. He was last known to be in the Wellington area according to his Life360 app. Billy's Silver 2014 GMC Sierra 1500 with Arkansas license plate # ASU88V has not been located.

McCrory (AR) Police Department Case #: 25-00054

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found December 23, 1912, in an old shack near the Provo Pressed Brickyard (Van Gundy's Brickyard) in the area of 200 W 1400 N, Vineyard/Provo.
The man was estimated to be about 45 years old, about 6' tall, and weighed about 150 lbs.  The man had a sandy complexion, a "Roman nose" and a moustache. .
He was found wearing only a shirt, undershirt, and one sock. There was also an overcoat located near the makeshift bed. Police theorized others may have found his body prior to reporting and his other clothing and items of value were stolen.
The man was never identified and was buried on December 29, 1912 in the Provo City Cemetery.

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found July 28th, 1930, about a mile west of the Midlake station on the Southern Pacific railroad trestle over the Great Salt Lake (known as the Lucin Cutoff). Location is approximate.
Only a right leg with a sock and brown oxford shoe was located. It was apparent that a transient had been riding a lumber car and lost his balance when the load shifted, and slipped under the wheels, severing his leg. A pair of crutches were found on the lumber car and the car behind it was covered in blood.  The accident was recent as the blood was still fresh at the site. It was theorized that the rest of his body fell into the Great Salt Lake, but it was never found despite searches by boat.
The leg was buried at Saline.

people.name: Baby John Doe
people.narrative: Found July 25, 1932, in the Jordan Canal about 100 yards south of the North Temple street bridge. The baby was a white male, about two months old and had been dead about nine months.

people.name: Baby John Doe
people.narrative: Found August 7, 1933 in the garbage dump at 45th South and State Street, Murray. The baby had been dead about a week and was wrapped in a partially burned sheet.

people.name: John Doe - Davis County
people.narrative:
Found August 7th, 1934, the day after the wreck of a Denver & Rio Grande Western Railroad train near the West Kaysville station, about a mile west of Kaysville. Locaction on map is approximate. He had been riding the mostly empty train southbound to Salt Lake City with about 20 other transients when the accident occurred.
John Doe was badly managled and the only description give was that of a white male, between 18 to 25 years old. A scrap of paper in his pocket read "Bar Joe Elwood" on one side and "Junction City, Ore., William E. Miller" on the other. He was never identified. He was buried on August 9th, 1934 in the Bountiful Cemetery.

people.name: Baby John Doe
people.narrative: Found August 26th, 1934, buried in a box in a field near Thirteenth East and Social Ave in Union; location on map is approximate. He was badly decomposed and may have been dead up to 8 weeks.

people.name: Baby John Doe
people.narrative: Found about 8 a.m. May 13, 1937 in a trash can near the tool shack of Lester Park, Ogden. A care taker emptying the trash noticed the body in a paper hat sack. The location was just across the street from Weber College. The baby boy was likely placed there within 24 hours of being discovered.

people.name: John Doe - Box Elder
people.narrative: Found January 19, 1939 by a sheepherder in a desert wash about 26 miles southwest of Kelton, Box Elder County; location on map is approximate. The remains were that of a man about 55 to 70 years old and 5'10' tall. He wore a brown coat and pants with a striped overall jacket. His shoes were badly worn. His pockets contains several highly colored rocks, 25 cents, a wrapper from a pound of butter and Jones saloon check. It was estimated he had been dead since February or March of 1938.

people.name: John Doe - Davis County
people.narrative:
Found August 21st, 1939, on Hwy 91 at the Farmington Junction after being struck by a vehicle while standing on the side of the road hitchhiking. He was killed instantly (though his face was recognizable). The hitchhiker was standing on the edge of the road, trying to get a ride southward.
The man was estimated to be 25 to 30 years old, about 5’ 9” tall, weighed between 170 and 180 lbs, had red close-cropped hair, light blue eyes, and a tattoo on his left forearm.
He was found wearing a gray checkered cap, two pairs of trousers: the outer pair was in army style being held up with a piece of rope, the inner pair was dark blue with a light blue stripe, and a grayish blue coat. His pockets contained a “number of trinkets” but no identification. He had 15 broken buttons, a pair of buck-skin gloves, an extra pair of socks, and a clipping from a Portland, Oregon newspaper dated Aug 12th.
Fingerprints were taken and sent to the FBI in Washington DC. They found no match. The man was never identified and was buried on August 30, 1939, in the Bountiful City Cemetery.

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative: Found July 27, 1940 in the stock yards of the Union Pacific Railroad company off Sugar Factory Road, Lehi; location on map is approximate. He had apparently been dead about four or five days as a merchant remembered selling the man three bottles of rubbing alcohol. The man was described as having a slight build, a dark complexion, a small mustache, a tattoo on the inside of his left leg and a scar on his left hip.

people.name: John Doe - Juab County
people.narrative:
Found April 25, 1941, along US HWY 189 (now known as U-132) in a patch of oak brush. He was located about 2.5 to 3 miles from Nephi, midway between the U. S. Gypsum company plant and the Nephi municipal power plant. Location on map is approximate.

Juab County Sheriff's Office Case #: 23JC0322

people.name: John Doe - Weber County
people.narrative: Found December 2, 1941, in an abandoned railroad right-of-way between the Union Pacific and Denver & Rio Grande Western tracks west of Nye's Corner near Roy, Weber County. He was located lying underneath a slicker with his shoes nearby next to the remains of a fire. It appeared as if he died in his sleep and had been there for several days. His clothing indicated he was likely a transient and he had a slip of paper in his pocket that showed he had recently been in Pocatello, Idaho. The man was about 50 to 60 years old, short and heavy set.

people.name: Baby John Doe
people.narrative: On February 15, 1942, a retired policeman collecting driftwood on the Jordan river found the body of a newborn near 1118 West Fourth Street South, about 125 feet north of the Fourth South street bridge (location on map is approximate). The body, that of a healthy baby boy weighing 8 to 9 pounds, was found inside a large brown paper sack. A piece of Salt Lake newspaper dated Feb 13 was found partially wrapped around the outside of the paper bag. An autopsy estimated the baby died about four to six weeks prior. A woman confessed to putting the baby of her daughter in the river. The baby was never identified and was buried in the Salt Lake City Cemetery.

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found June 16, 1943, by a sheepherder about 2.5 miles northeast of Soldiers Summit. He was laying on the mountain side in the open near a mountain trail (map location is approximate).
Some broken twigs and a spot where the ground had been slightly trampled was found about 70 feet above the body, but no tracks were visible between there and the body.
There were some slight scratches on his legs, but no serious cuts or any broken bones noted. The body was too badly decomposed to determine a cause of death, and the time of death was estimated to be about 2 months prior to discovery. He was wearing only ragged underwear.
The man was estimated to be middle-aged, African-American, possibly between 40-50 years old, about 5’ 5” tall and weighed about 125 pounds. The man was never identified. On June 18 or 19, 1943, he was buried in the Heber City Cemetery.

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found September 8, 1944, on the salt flats west of Kaysville by two boys out riding motorcycles.. Location on map is approximate.
The man was described as about 65 years old, a few inches over 5' with gray or partially grey hair and no teeth present.
He was dressed in faded green plaid trousers, red suspenders, a white t-shirt, and underwear. There was an empty eyeglasses case found near the body. Belief that he is of Japanese ancestry is theoretical.

Davis County Sheriff's Office Case #: D20-5810

people.name: Baby John Doe
people.narrative: Found in a metal bucket on October 28, 1945, caught in the willows along the Jordan River near 450 Goshen St.. The body was found wrapped in strips of newspaper.

people.name: Baby John Doe
people.narrative:
Found February 15, 1947, by three boys playing in Mill Race near the Provo Gold Course stuck in a clump of willows  Location on map is approximate.
The baby boy was described as white/Caucasian and well developed. He was very badly decomposed. It was noted that there were marks of violence on the body. The physician estimated that the baby had been in the water for about one to two weeks. The baby remained unidentified and was buried in the Provo City Cemetery. .

Provo City Police Department Case #: 22PR08066

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found April 26, 1949, in the "hobo jungles" next to the Denver and Rio Grande Railroad line near Spring Glen. Location on map is approximate.
Locals recognized him as a man that camped there a few nights with another transient, and they had given the pair some food. The other transient was questioned and admitted he had a meal with the dead man Sunday night and ate breakfast with him Monday morning. He said the unidentified Elderly Man was asleep Monday when he left him. It was reported that the unidentified man mentioned he had visited Telluride, Colorado, and that he was from Kentucky, near the Tennessee border. Fingerprints were taken and sent to the FBI, but he was never identified. On May 2, he was buried in the Carbonville Cemetery.
The man was described as elderly, about 50 years (it says 52 on the death certificate), although a local said that the man had told him he was 69 years old. He was Caucasian and had no identification papers in his clothes. No height or weight was reported for the unidentified man. The cause of death was listed as “Arteriosclerotic Heart Disease,” and there were no marks of violence on his body. He had died within 24 hours of being found.

Carbon County Sheriff's Office Case #: 20220402

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found on September 20th, 1951, on the Denver and Rio Grande Western Railroad near the Roy Station just outside Roy, south of Roy Road (now 97). The man had been knocked from the top of a flat top freight car when the train reduced speed and was ran over by following 28 freight cars.
No wallet or papers were found, but food for a lunch was found on top of a flat car loaded with lumber. A fingerprint and thumb print were sent to the FBI, but he was never identified.
The unidentified man was described as a white/Caucasian man, between 50 and 55 years old. He stood about 58", weighed about 160 pounds, and had dark graying hair. He wore a blue pin-striped suit.
His remains were sent to for removal/disposal to the University of Utah on Sept 25, 1951.

Weber County Sheriff's Office Case #: 23WC11744

people.name: Baby John Doe
people.narrative:
Found March 1, 1952, in the Vernal city dump at 500 N 1500 E, near a gravel pile on the west end. The body was wrapped in layers of heavy brown paper that was partially burned away, however, no burning had taken place in the dump recently. The body itself was slightly burned on the back and right leg. There were no other marks on the body. Dr. Ray Spendlove stated that the baby appeared to have been born alive and normal, had weighed between 7-8 pounds, and was about 18 inches long. The Death Certificate describes the baby as white, but a newspaper article noted the baby was of a dark complexion. How long the baby had been in the dump could not be determined since the body was frozen solid, but it could have been up to several weeks. He was never identified and was buried March 15, 1952 in the Vernal Town Cemetery (now Vernal Memorial Park).

Uintah County Sheriff's Office Case #: S20-2388

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found April 26, 1953, unconscious on the second story at the Capitol Hotel at 76 W 2nd S, Salt Lake City. The man died enroute to the Salt Lake County General Hospital. An autopsy determined the man had been stabbed near the center of the chest which pierced his heart. The man’s two companions were booked into the County Jail for questioning. The trio had just arrived from Portland, Oregon when they checked into the Hotel, drank, and then went to bed. According to a witness, when the trio awoke, Ellis Olie Hazelwood, 64, discovered his money missing and accused John Doe of stealing it. John Doe then grabbed Hazelwood in a “serious” gesture which caused Hazelwood to stab him once in the chest. Meanwhile, the police attempted to identify the dead man. He checked in under the name Johnny Thompson, but his companions knew him as Johnny Beaukins. Paper in his possession gave the Name John F Hansen of 26 N W St. Portland. Police contacted authorities in Portland. They said a man matching that description spent time in a Washington State Prison at Walla Walla, WA. Salt Lake Police obtained John Hansen’s next of kin’s information. Mr and Mrs A. R. Hansen of Aberdeen, Idaho, viewed the body at the Desert Mortuary in Salt Lake City and said the body was not their son. Ellis Hazelwood was convicted of Voluntary Manslaughter in the man’s death. The unidentified man's fingerprints were sent to the FBI, but he was never positively identified. His body was sent to the University of Utah for disposal.

Salt Lake City Police Department Case #: 1953-38118

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found July 15th, 1954, wedged against a water gate near its diversion from the Jordan River in the surplus canal near 1200 W 2100 S, Salt Lake City. The body was discovered by a man living near the junction of the canal and the Jordan River. The man told police he crossed the gate often, and the body was not there the day before.
The body was covered in mud and clad in only a pair of athletic shorts. He was described as a white/Caucasian middle-aged man, probably between 30-40 years old. He was about 5' 10" tall and weighed about 165 pounds.
An autopsy determined that the unidentified man suffered a heart attack. There was no water in his lungs and no signs of foul play. Salt Lake County Deputy Sheriff found socks, shoes, a shirt, and some bedding on the Jordan River bank approximately a block south of where the body was found. Police compared fingerprints and thought they found a match, but the dental records failed to match. Fingerprints were forwarded to the FBI, but the man was never identified. He was buried on July 16, 1954, in the Salt Lake City Cemetery.

people.name: John Doe - Iron County
people.narrative:
Found April 15, 1955, hanging in a juniper tree about 3 miles southwest of Parowan, opposite Hwy 91 in a heavily wooded area. Location on map is approximate.
Witnesses said they had seen the man walking on the road between Parowan and Summit near where his body was found earlier in the week. A three man coroner’s jury determined it was likely a suicide.
The man was described as Hispanic/Latino and likely around 60 years old. He was found with a bandana.
Fingerprints were sent to the FBI, but the man was never identified. The man was buried in the Cedar City Cemetery on April 25, 1955.

people.name: John Doe - Box Elder
people.narrative:
Found September 11, 1955 on the shore of Gunnison Island in the Great Salt Lake, about 36 miles from Promontory Point. Location on map is approximate. On October 2, more remains were located and the area screened/sifted. 10 buttons, bits of cloth and a metal fastener from a pair of Lee bib overalls, and jumper were recovered.
The man was described as about 5' 5" tall. He may have had an amputated leg. Also noted was extensive loss of teeth several years before his death, suggesting he had very little dental care except for possible extractions.
The leading theory was this John Doe was the rest of the body of a severed leg found on the Sothern Pacific trestle in 1930, however, this has not been confirmed.

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative: Found in early June 1956 by children and their teacher out on a field trip in Pleasant Valley near Myton, Duchesne County; location on map is approximate. The remains were not a complete skeleton but were described as being those of a white adult male, 35 to 40 years old, and 63 to 67 inches tall.

people.name: John Doe - Salt Lake
people.narrative:
Found July 4, 1959, by two hikers face down in the creek about three-fourths of a mile from the mouth of City Creek Canyon, near the Salt Lake City business district.
The man had been in the creek for about a week according to the pathologist that examined the man. There were no indications of foul play and authorities believed it was likely a suicide.
Though there were two bottles of sleeping tablets and a white clothesline in the tree, there was no indication that they were used. The man had a wallet, but it carried no identification. He did have a hat that was from Chicago and a pencil with the name "Denver Plastics, Golden, Colo" on it. He also carried a paper that indicated he traveled by bus. Authorities contacted Golden, Colorado Police to inquire about any missing persons but did not turn up any leads.
He was described as a white male over the age of 50, about 55 years old, 6' to 6' 1" tall, and weight 173 pounds.
Fingerprints were obtained from the man and sent to the FBI while a composite sketch of this John Doe was released to the public. He was never identified. On July 7th, 1959, John Doe was buried in the Salt Lake City Cemetery.

people.name: John Doe - Weber
people.narrative:
Found May 21st, 1959, lying face down on an improvised bed of cardboard in the railroad yards between W 33rd and W 36th St., Ogden.  The man was described as about 60-65 years of age and had been dead for about two or three hours. There was no identification on the body, but 50 cents and a few other possessions were found in his pockets.
It was determined there were no signs of violence or foul play, and the death was considered to be from natural causes. Fingerprints were checked locally as well as sent to the FBI, but the man was never identified. The unknown man was buried Jun 1 or 2, 1959, in the Ogden City Cemetery under a headstone reading “Tramp.”

Weber County Sheriff's Office Case #: 23WC11745

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found March 26th, 1960, three Boy Scouts hiking in City Creek Canyon about 1.5 miles from the gate at the mouth of the canyon and about 75 yards from the road in a small ravine.
His fists were clenched over his stomach and his head was laying on a rock. Investigators theorized the body could've been there since the previous autumn or as little as two to three months.
The man was described as 5' 1" tall, of medium build, and had a grey beard and mustache. He was wearing a blue pinstriped suit, black high top shoes, and a blue work shirt. The coat pocket had the initials BA while a ring the man wore had the initials R G.
Investigators checked with dental offices, jewelry stores, and even VA hospitals to try to identify him. Composites drawings of the man were made and circulated to other agencies including the FBI. He was buried in the Salt Lake City Cemetery on March 30th, 1960.

people.name: Baby John Doe
people.narrative:
Found April 16th, 1960, in the mail at 2212 E 3020 S, Salt Lake City. The parcel from Dallas, Texas, was delivered by the USPS. The address on the box did not exist, but the mail carrier recognized the name on the box and delivered it to their house. The residents thought that the parcel was an Easter gift for a relative and did not open it until the next day. When the egg box measuring 12in x 10in x 8in was opened, it contained the body of a dead baby boy wrapped in aluminum foil. The residents notified the Salt Lake County Sheriff who in turn involved the FBI, postal authorities, and Dallas Police.
The baby was Caucasian with red to reddish brown hair and weighed 10 pounds.
He had a bruise on his back and the back of his head. A pathologist determined the baby had been born alive after a normal delivery. The cause of death was asphyxiation and was likely dead before being sent in the mail. The FBI and postal authorities bowed out of the investigation citing no jurisdiction and the only crime committed would be sending a deceased body without proper paperwork.
The return address was traced to a widow in Dallas who had no information about a baby or who would’ve sent the package with her name on it. The box and wrappings were sent to Dallas Police for further investigation. The baby was never identified and was buried either April 27 or 28 in the Salt Lake City Cemetery.

people.name: Baby John Doe
people.narrative:
Found March 23, 1963, about 2 miles up Mill Creek Canyon by a father and son hiking in the area.
They noticed a yellow towel sticking out under some rocks and when they removed the rocks, they found the body. Another towel, a pillowcase and a sheet were also found near the body.
The baby was described as a white/Caucasian boy that weighed about 7 pounds.
An autopsy determined the baby had been born alive after a full gestation and was perfect. The cause of death was from poor care and hemorrhage from a crudely cut umbilical cord. The baby was likely less than 24 hours old when it was placed in the canyon.
The sheriff’s office conducted an investigation, but the baby was never identified. He was buried on April 6, 1963, in the Salt Lake City Cemetery.

Unified Police Department of Greater Salt Lake Case #: CO 2021-132388

people.name: John Doe - Iron County
people.narrative:
Found July 16, 1965, by two park rangers checking a climbing area on the edge of Cedar Breaks cliffs near Sunset View in the Cedar Breaks National Monument. The man was between two logs, doubled-over, about 300 feet from the highway. He was nearly out of sight.
$1.51 and a flashlight were found on the body while an empty wallet, an empty metal suitcase, and a cane were found nearby. There was no identification of any kind.
The body was sent to the state medical examiner in Salt Lake City. It was determined that the body was about 5 feet 9, slender, and between 50 and 60 years old. The man would’ve worn an 8.5 D shoe size.
Foul play was also indicated though a cause of death could not be determined.

Iron County Sheriff's Office Case #: 65-0001

people.name: John Doe - Box Elder
people.narrative:
Found August 22, 1965, about 100 mi west of Brigham City, laying in a foot of water. He was near Groome's Siding, west of Promontory Point, along a Southern Pacific railroad track in a marshy area.
The body was described as an African American male, about 30 years old, with a beard. He was about 5' 10" tall and weighed 130 pounds. It appeared that there was no foul play and he likely fell from a train  about a day prior. He had multiple head injuries, including a skull fracture.
Investigators learned that the man boarded the train in California and had purchased a loaf of bread in Elko, NV. Several attempts were made to take fingerprints from the man though it was difficult due to the condition of his skin after being submerged in the water for some time. The man was never identified and was buried in the Brigham City Cemetery on August 27, 1965.

Box Elder County Sheriff's Office Case #: D-348

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found July 13, 1966, by fishermen caught in a brush and log jam in the Weber River west of the Ogden turkey plant, approximately 2650 South, Ogden. Location on map is approximate.
The body was badly decomposed, and a cause of death could not be determined. It was estimated he had been dead about three months. His face was not recognizable, and fingerprints could not be taken. The body appeared to be that of an adult male between the ages of 20 to 35 years old. He had dark black hair, a large forehead and large eyes. His ethnicity was described as both white or American Indian/Alaskan Native.
Dental charts from the US Army were sent to the police to compare to John Doe, however, he was edentulous. The man was never identified and was cremated on July 28th, 1966 at the Aultorest Memorial Park in Ogden.

Ogden City Police Department Case #: 24g13268

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found September 1, 1967, by fishermen in the brush by the Colorado River just west of the Colorado/Utah border near the town of Westwater. Location on map is approximate.
It is estimated the body had been there about two to four months. Evidence at the scene led investigators to believe the body was thrown from a truck. A wallet laid about six feet from the body. The wallet contained an ID for Everett Laysbad, a Native American from the Sioux Indian Reservation at Porcupine, South Dakota. Authorities in Grand Junction, Colorado stated that Mr. Laysbad was known to them and was on probation for auto theft. He was last seen about June 27th in the Grand Junction area. Sheriff Haskell Bowman said there were possible indications of foul play: a crack between the eyes and a hole at the back of the skull, possibly from a bullet. The body was sent to the State Medical Examiners office for further investigation and positive identification. In May 1968, almost nine months later, it was announced that the body was NOT that of Everett Laysbad. It was the opinion of the assistant pathologist that the skull was not that of a Native American, and jewelry that Mr. Laysbad was known to wear was absent from the body. It is unknown what happened to the unidentified body after this point.

Grand County Sheriff's Office Case #: 2100588

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found August 13, 1973, on a sandbar in the Bear River about a mile north of the SR102 crossing near Deweyville.
The man was only wearing a pair of shorts.
He was described as possibly Native American or Hispanic/Latino and about 20-35 years old although one newspaper article says he could be from 12 to 40 years old. The man had black hair, stood 5' 2" to 5' 3" tall, and weighed around 130 pounds. His teeth were in perfect condition with no cavities or restorations. There were no tattoos, scars, or marks on the body.
An autopsy determined the man likely drowned and there were no signs of foul play. It was estimated he was dead more than a week.
He was badly decomposed, but authorities were able to take two fingerprints that were sent to the FBI. However, there were no matches found. A lead was uncovered when an East Tremonton woman said a man matching John Doe's description came to her door asking for a drink of water.  He was never identified and was buried in the Brigham City Cemetery on August 17, 1973.

Box Elder County Sheriff's Office Case #: 73-000427

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found February 1, 1976, about 3 miles east of Green River near (old) US Hwy 50-6 by two men out riding motorcycles. Location on map is approximate. Some sources say John Doe was found 3 miles WEST of Green River.
The man was described as white/Caucasian, between 30-35 years old, about 5' 11" tall with dark brown hair that was 7" long. He also had a beard and moustache and was noted as being "powerfully built."
The body was found with a pair of Levi-Strauss blue denim pants with a 29" inseam, a 36" long dark brown belt, and Royal Coach brand cotton briefs (size medium, 34"-36"). He carried two sets of identification from Texas. Also found was $860 and some Mexican currency.
Investigators tracked down the family from the identification, but they indicated John Doe was not their relative. He was never identified and was buried in the Castle Dale Cemetery.

Emery County Sheriff's Office Case #: 20-00519

people.name: John Doe - Salt Lake
people.narrative:
Found June 24, 1978, after a fire at the Rio Grande Building Products, 540 W 200 S, Salt Lake City.
The only clothing found with the victim was one burned shoe. A bottle of Thunderbird was next to the decedent's body. Three packs and a sleeping bag were also found nearby. There was no identification or jewelry found.
The man was described as white/Caucasian, likely in his late 30's to early 40's, between 5' 6" and 6' tall and probably weighed about 150 pounds.
An autopsy determined the man died of carbon monoxide poisoning and burns and was heavily intoxicated when he died. There was also evidence that he suffered an infection in his right lung at one time in his life.
The man only had three teeth remaining, and there was no evidence he ever received dental work, making a dental comparison doubtful. There were no fingerprints able to be taken.

Salt Lake City Police Department Case #: 1978-46921

people.name: John Doe - Carbon County
people.narrative:
Found September 20, 1978, at the bottom of an empty coal car by workers at the Banning Station near the Sunnyside Junction, about 17 miles east of Price. Location on map is approximate.
On September 9, the coal car had been in Grand Island, Nebraska. The train then traveled through Salt Lake City, Provo and Helper before arriving to rural Carbon County on September 18, 1978.
The man carried a backpack and several items which gave clues to his prior movements. He was carrying a pair of prescription eyeglasses and a case with "B Botions Sullivan Union National Building Wichita, Kansas" written on it. There was a book of matches from County Steel Liquor Store and a second book of matches from Westgate Bank, both in Lincoln, Nebraska. He also had an empty vodka bottle from Utah State Liquor Store, and there was also a bottle of hair lotion with the name "Byron Ditton" on it.
The man is believed to be white/Caucasian although a newspaper articled noted he may be Native American. He was probably between 50 to 55 years old, stood 5' 5" tall, and weighed 158 pounds. He had grey to partially grey short hair and burn scar on his right thigh. There were also numerous tattoos: snake on right arm; "Dorothy Jan 8 1950" on right forearm; dagger on right forearm; two daggers on chest. He was likely dead only a few days.
Fingerprints were taken and sent to the FBI, but he was never identified.

Carbon County Sheriff's Office Case #: 19780920

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found June 9, 1979 by railroad crews laying beside the railroad tracks near the 2100 S railroad overpass at 670 W, Salt Lake City. Location on map is approximate.
The man was found with his trousers around his ankles and had been stabbed in the back of his thighs. An autopsy determined the man died of blood loss and shock and estimated the man had been dead approximately 3 hours before he was found.
Detectives stated the man was likely a transient who had been hanging around the railroad tracks. Documents found on the body and his fingerprints were sent to the FBI for comparison. But the FBI found that the man’s fingerprints did not match the name on the documents. The man was never identified and was buried on June 22, 1979 in the Taylorsville Cemetery.

people.name: Baby John Doe
people.narrative: Found about 11:22 p.m. January 29, 1981, in a sink in the ladies' restroom on the bottom floor of St. Benedict's Hospital at 5475 S 500 E, Washington Terrace. The baby boy was wrapped in a white sheet and the cause of death was unknown.

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found June 15, 1981, nude on the bank of a 3 foot deep irrigation canal about 1.5 miles west and .5 miles north of Bear River City. Location on map is approximate.
The medical examiner determined the man likely took his own life. The multiple stab wounds to his abdomen were self-inflicted and superficial. He was likely only dead a few days.
The man was described as white/Caucasian and between the ages of 20 to 40. He stood at 5' 8" tall and weighed 177 pounds. The man had red or auburn hair, long reddish-brown sideburns, a reddish-brown mustache, and green eyes.
His fingerprints were sent to the FBI, but he was never identified. He was buried on July 10, 1981 in the Brigham City Cemetery.

Box Elder County Sheriff's Office Case #: 81-0433

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found March 13, 1982, floating in Jordan River surplus canal by a couple canoeing west of the airport near 540 W Harold Gatty Dr., Salt Lake City. Location on map is approximate.
The man was wearing Levi blue jeans, a heavy coat with wool lining, a black or brown and white vertically striped shirt with a western design, jockey shorts, and long wool socks. No shoes were located. He also wore a silver Seiko digital wrist watch made in 1980. It was still working and was an hour ahead of the local time in Salt Lake City. The man had a wire around his neck, but it did not appear to cause any injury. He also carried a blue bandana.
An examination indicated lower back surgery. The incision was on the front part of his body just below the navel. There was also a linear scar extending from right upper abdominal quadrant vertically past the umbilicus on the right to the suprapubic region. Faint linear scars on back of left wrist and forearm were noted as well. He had been in the canal for several months.
He was descried as white/Caucasian, between the ages of 25 to 35 with brown eyes. He stood 5' 4" tall and weighed 145 pounds. His hair was dark brown, medium length, with moderate graying on the sides and back. The man had a black or dark brown mustache and abundant dark brown or black hair over his torso and back.
Fingerprints and dental charts are available.

Salt Lake City Police Department Case #: 1982-20551

people.name: John Doe - Summit County
people.narrative:
Found June 24, 1983, buried under rocks and branches in a wooded area between Scow Lake and Christmas Meadows Campground (about 1 to 1 3/4 mile from the Stillwater Trailhead) by two forest service rangers. The area was on the east side of the Bear River about 25 miles due east of Wanship. Location on map is approximate.
He was killed by a .22 caliber gunshot to the back of the head. Investigators theorized the body has been there since November 1982. Identification was made difficult because animals gnawed the upper half of the body.
The man was described as being of unknown ethnicity, between the ages of 23-33 years old and about 5' 4" tall. He had course, black, wavy hair.
The body was found with light brown cords over dark brown pants, jockey shorts, multi colored shirt. There was a fatigue jacket around the arms and chest.
The unidentified man carried a note that read:
'MARLSHIA 268-1473
KAKALA Brooklin Av 116 Salt
Carl Dearden 855-2341
Home 272-5093"
Investigators believed they had a possible identity for John Doe, who was Hispanic. They had dental charts from penitentiaries in Arizona and Colorado checked as it was believed the victim spent time there.

Summit County Sheriff's Office Case #: 83-178

people.name: John Doe - Millard County
people.narrative:
Found December 28, 1983, roughly 30 yards from the Sevier River bank about 2 miles west of Deseret by a rancher. Location on map is approximate.
Based on the location and decomposition of the skull, investigators theorized this was the remains of a transient that that witnesses saw fall while attempting to cross the flooded Sevier River in June 1983. The transient's traveling companion indicated while he didn't know the man's name, he was about 35 years old and had said he was from Pennsylvania.
The unidentified remains were described as belonging to a white/Caucasian male, about 20-30 years old, although one newspaper article said he could be 20 to 40 years old. There was about 2.5 cm of reddish hair attached to the lower left jaw. The man had extensive dental work.

Millard County Sheriff's Office Case #: 12-715-83

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found November 6, 1985, near the Colorado River, washed ashore at Westwater Ranch. It was estimated that the skeletal remains were there for about a year, but one newspaper article estimates up to two years.
The remains were described as a non-Hispanic or Latino male (the anthropology reports differ on whether he was white/Caucasian or Black/African American). He stood about 5' 4" to 5' 7" tall and was probably 45 to 55 years old.

Grand County Sheriff's Office Case #: 851186

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found September 15, 1987, near a stream in Malan's Basin between Taylor Canyon and Waterfall Canyon. Location on map is approximate.
Only 11 bones were found in total; the skull was not among them. The man was described as white/Caucasian, between 30 to 50 years old, about 5' 7" tall and possibly weighed around 160 to 185 pounds. He was possibly bow-legged and had fine facial features. The man was near-sighted.
Found with the body was one pair of glasses of gold partial wire rim frames with dark lenses, one pair of glasses of gold partial wire rim frames with clear lenses (prescription: right 50-0.25-12 left 25-25-60) and a hard-shell glasses case with velvet lining. Both of the glasses were made by Bosch and Lomb after 1945.
The remains of what was probably the man's clothing included three plastic buttons and a zipper closure with the words, "USA" and "Talon" stamped on it. The soles of two shoes, measuring 29 cm (US men's size 11) were also located.
Some of the property found with the body included a nail clippers, a leather wallet, six metal coins dated 1935 to 1956, a pen with a plastic barrel, one .32 caliber Remington ACP cartridge and one .45 caliber un-deformed, unjacketed round-nose projectile (rifling marks have left hand twist).

Weber County Sheriff’s Office Case #: 87WC6824

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found April 17, 1990, after an accidental fire in a boarded up duplex at 846 S Washington St., Salt Lake City. He was staying there with two other transients drinking alcohol, and when they fell asleep, a candle was left burning. The house caught on fire, and John Doe did not escape.
The two other transients told investigators the decedent was known as "Kentucky" but that his first name may have been Charles or Chuck and he was from Louisville, Kentucky.
The man was described as white/Caucasian and about 5' 5" tall. The original medical examiner's report does not say how old the John Doe may have been, only that he was an adult.
The only clothing found were the remains of a charred turquoise sweatshirt.

Salt Lake City Police Department Case #: 90-035537

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found at the Road Home Shelter at 210 S Rio Grande St., Salt Lake City, where he was arrested and taken to jail. John Doe suffered a heart attack from a drug overdose and stopped breathing while in a holding cell. He was taken to LDS hospital and later died without giving his name.
The man was measured at 5' 9" tall and 176 pounds. He was estimated to be between 30 to 50 years old. He had brown eyes, a black mustache, and thick, black wavy hair. The man also had wavy dark hair on his chest along with the tattoo of a holy figure holding a chalice. A tattoo of the word, "Nury" on posterior proximal right upper arm.
His race or ethnicity is unknown, however, it was noted that he was likely of mixed with possible Black/African American and Hispanic ancestry. He spoke both English and Spanish.

Salt Lake City Police Department Case #: 1993-109622

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found June 24, 1996, floating in the Colorado River near the Westwater Ranger station. A ranger working the station saw the body float by and caught up with it about 3.5 miles down river.
The man was fully dressed in a short sleeved, striped shirt, dark corduroy pants held up by a belt, socks and shoes of unknown description. He was wearing a 'Wilson' brand digital watch and had wet, torn papers in his pockets.
He was described as white/Caucasian, between the ages of 20 to 60, about 5' 8" tall and estimated to be 112 pounds. His black hair was 2 cm long. He also had black beard and mustache stubble. The man had a tattoo of a heart with unidentifiable markings in the middle of it on the lateral aspect of his left upper arm.

Grand County Sheriff's Office Case #: 96-0430

people.name: John Doe - Washington County
people.narrative:
Found December 7, 1998, in a small cave about 200 feet from the top of Webb Hill, St. George, by three teenagers exploring the area. Part of the cave had a stacked stone wall to enclose it.
The body was found wearing several layers of clothing: two shirts (one handmade, one manufactured), a burlap vest with repairs, a jacket and denim pants. The pants had buttons with possibly a star design. He also wore a pair of model No. 316 boots sold by Marshall Fields from 1917 to 1920 with very little wear on the bottoms.
Three blankets were also located: one blue wool blanket, one thin cotton woven, and one homemade patchwork quilt with a blue patch sewn to repair a hole.
John Doe was described as being between 15-17 years old, and a forensic anthropologist is quoted saying the teeth indicated he was likely 15.1 to 15.9 years old. There was tobacco staining present meaning he was a smoker or tobacco user. He was white/Caucasian with some possible Native American features. He stood between 5' 4" and 5' 6' tall.
Examination of his remains showed he had a partially healed left hand fracture and a thirteenth right rib. A well-developed right arm showed extensive use and indicated he most likely was a heavy industry worker. This was common at the time, but unusual to see in a child. John Doe had an active pulmonary infection, but it is unknown if this played a role in his death.

St. George Police Department Case #: 9807859

people.name: John Doe - Beaver County 1
people.narrative:
Found April 13, 2004, approximately half a mile northwest of the Milford Municipal Airport in a sagebrush-covered prairie by a couple on horseback.
Clothing was still present with the body including a camouflage-patterned jacket with military insignias on both upper arms (size small/short), a cotton V-neck t-shirt, white cotton men's briefs (Hanes brand, waist 32/34), black 'Solo and Semore' brand denim jeans (size 38, too big for the decedent), a black leather belt cinched to the last notch with a plain metal buckle, white ribbed sports socks. The belt had bright blue paint spattered on the front. The man also wore size 10 ankle high, steel-toed boots that also bright blue paint spattered on top of the toe of the left boot. Ten coins found in the pockets of the pants dated from 1979 to 1996.
He was described as Asian (possibly Vietnamese) about 39 to 60 years old although an examine noted he could be 50 to 56. He had black hair, was estimated to be 5' 6" tall and weighed around 120 pounds.
There were two fractures to skull consistent with blunt force trauma; he is considered a homicide victim. Dental Records are available for comparison.

Beaver County Sheriffs Office Case #: OCA17459

people.name: John Doe - Beaver County 2
people.narrative:
Found April 26, 2004, by hunters in a dry creek bed about four miles west of Beaver and one mile north of State Road 21. Location on map is approximate.
The body wore a 'Columbia' brand light gray parka with navy blue inserts, (size large), a white long sleeved dress shirt with a mandarin collar ('UTY' brand, size medium), a white cotton-knit under shirt (size large), white fleece lined sweat pants ('Fruit of the Loom' brand, size medium), cotton-knit ribbed sports socks, and Hanes brand briefs (size 30 waist). No shoes were located, but an ACE bandage was worn by the victim, indicating a possible injury.
He was described as Hispanic/Latino, between 33 to 42 years old with a small build. He stood around 5' 4" to 5' 5" tall and had black hair with a slight curl.
It is likely he had been dead for several years. Dental records are available for comparison.

Beaver County Sheriffs Office Case #: 17568

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found June 27, 2010, by hikers off of Mirror Lake Highway (UT-150) near Bald Mountain in the Uinta Mountains; location on map is approximate. The only thing found was part of a skull.
The remains are that of a pre-40 year old adult. Sex, race, and height could not be determined.

Summit County Sheriff's Office Case #: 10-L11247

people.name: John Doe - Tooele County
people.narrative:
Found May 15, 2011, among heavy sage brush by a local rancher checking spring runoff in a field near Stockton. Location on map is approximate.
Blue jeans with a black nylon belt with white skulls and a metal buckle with the imprint of a marijuana leaf were found around the bottom half of the body. A Santa Muerte pendant was also located.
The remains showed signs of mild to moderate Schmorl’s nodes on the inferior surface of T7-T9. This condition is associated with ruptured or injured inter vertebral discs and is uncommon. The presence of these nodes could suggest intensive, habitual activity that caused injury to the lower back region.
The man was described as Hispanic/Latino and between the ages of 18 to 35. He had black hair, and his height was estimated to be 4' 11' to 5' 6" The man had likely been dead about three years prior to discovery.

Tooele County Sheriff's Office Case #: 2011-002439

people.name: John Doe - Millard County
people.narrative:
Found December 16, 2011, partially submerged in water near the south shore of Sevier Lake by geologists working on a drilling rig. Location on map is approximate.
Assorted clothing found with the body included: a 'Stretch" brand red button-down shirt with a collar (size 26/28, no logo); a black ski cap with 'Cal' written on the front and 'Golden Bears' on the back; a 'Club Athlete' brand black hoodie (size XL, no logo); 'a 'Fruit of the Loom' brand grey hoodie with a zippered front (size XXXL); Black Label' brand pants of unknown color (size 36X32); and a belt of unknown color.
The man was described as white/Caucasian, between 40 to 60 years old, around 5' 6" to 5' 11" tall and probably have a beard and mustache. The hair color could not be determined due to bleaching. He could have been dead up to 15 years prior to discovery.

Millard County Sheriff's Office Case #: 11-67926

people.name: John Doe
people.narrative:
Found May 7, 2012, by Utah Department of Transportation workers doing maintenance on the freeway overpass near I-15 and Riverdale Rd, Roy. He was in what appeared to be a makeshift camp underneath the overpass. Location on map is approximate.
Many locals recognized the man as local transient and some called him 'Terry.' A credible tip stated his name was Robert Terry Sant, and he was born in Florida, possibly near Kissimmee. Records in Florida were checked but nothing panned out.
The man was found wearing several articles of clothing: an 'Oakley' brand soft wear style jacket; a 'Timberlake' brand jacket (size L); a 'Port Authority' brand long sleeved shirt with 'Zupas' stitched on the pocket (size M); a 'Fruit of the Loom' brand shirt (size M); and 'Faded Glory' brand jeans (size 30x32).
He carried a newspaper dated March 8, 2012 and a UTA/Tracks pass dated March 21, 2012. An analog wrist watch with 'H-BAHGI' on the back was also found.
John Doe was described as white/Caucasian, between the ages of 40 to 50, and about 5' 6" tall. He had brown, wavy hair, approximately 16 cm long. A grey/brown, approximately 7 cm long, was also noted.
He had been dead about five weeks. No foul play is suspected. One thumbprint and dental charts are available for comparisons, but it should be noted the man did not receive dental work in the years prior to his death.

State Bureau of Investigations (SBI) Case #: 12DPSO477

people.name: John Doe - Grand County
people.narrative:
Found September 18, 2014, laying on a foam pad under a juniper tree by hikers ascending out of the central canyon on the west side of Hotel Mesa near Moab. Location on map is approximate.
The man was wearing a light colored shirt and tan camouflage cargo pants held up by a dark, leather belt. On his feet was a pair of blue hiking socks and 'Vasque' brand Gore-Tex high ankle brown hiking boots ( size 10). A camouflage colored, wide brimmed hat was also found near the body.
Also found close by was a leather gun holster belt with ammunition, green netting material, a yellow Bic lighter, a blue and white 'Thermarest' brand sleeping pad, a white and red handkerchief, and pieces of blue rope with 2 black hooks attached.
The man was described as 45 to 55 years old and of unknown race or ethnicity; possibly Asian, Native American, or Latino. He likely stood between 5' 5" and 5' 9" tall.

Grand County Sheriff's Office Case #: 2014000520

people.name: John Doe - Grand County
people.narrative:
Found December 6, 2014 in a desert wash, in a very rural area of Grand County;  location on map is approximate (exact location not released).
The remains are that of an adult, but age and race could not be determined. The remains were likely there between 1970 and 1980.

Grand County Sheriff's Office Case #: 2014000657

people.name: John Doe - Emery County
people.narrative:
Found April 1, 2020, in a remote desert area of Emery County. Location on map is approximate; exact location unknown/not released.
Items of clothing found with the body include a pair of denim pants (size 32" x 34" slim), a black belt, boxer shorts and black 'Undertech' brand underwear. A backpack was located near the body as well.
The man was described as white/Caucasian, around 25-35 years old, and stood between 6' 2" to 6' 4" tall.

Emery County Sheriff's Office Case #: 20-01552

people.name: John Doe - Iron County
people.narrative:
Found October 7, 2020, in an outcropping of rocks in Shurtz Canyon by a crew working for the Bureau of Land Management, doing trail maintenance work. The area was very remote and required an ATV to access it. Location on map is approximate; exact location not released.
Tan pants or shorts (unknown brand or size), a portion of a leather belt, and green leather shoes (unknown brand or size) were also found with the body.
The man was described as Hispanic/Latino and stood about 5' 2" to 5' 6" tall. The forensic anthropologist indicated the decedent was likely over 35 years of age at the time of his death. He appeared to have been there for some time.

Iron County Sheriff's Office Case #: unknown

people.name: John Doe - Washington County
people.narrative:
Located February 6, 2024 by antler shed hunters near the Red Mountain Trailhead in Washington County; exact location unknown/not released. The remains were nearly skeletonized and covered with two large trash bags. Additionally, there was a backpack under the skull.
A black shirt and blue sweater was found on the boddy as well as black pants with belt, black underwear, two black socks, and brown Hi-Tec boots. A pair of eyeglasses, a black Hurley hat, and two black knee braces.
He was estimated to be between the ages of 45-65 and was 5'7" to 6'2" tall.

Washington County Sheriff's Office Case #: 24W000955

people.name: John Doe - Weber County
people.narrative:
Found May 21, 2025, in a rural mountainous area of Weber County. Exact location not released; location on map is approximate.
The remains were skeletal parts only and some parts were not recovered. Near the body a size small plaid jacket and size 9 hiking books were also recovered.

Weber County Shriff's Office Case #: 25WC17608

people.name: Baby Jane Doe
people.narrative: Found October 30, 1928, in a shoebox on the sidewalk along University Ave, above Ninth North Street; location on map is approximate. The baby girl was wrapped in a pink nightgown and a women's handkerchief was also inside. The cause of death of blood loss and/or exposure. It was estimated the baby had been there less than 24 hours.

people.name: Baby Jane Doe
people.narrative: Found May 21, 1929 in a suitcase hidden in a clump of weeds by a man spraying a vacant lot near the corner of 2nd E and 4th S, Salt Lake City; location on map is approximate. The remains were that a of baby girl, wrapped in a silk kimono and two silk sport shirts of costly design. The remains were probably in the suitcase for a week to ten days.

people.name: Baby Jane Doe
people.narrative: Found September 26th, 1933, caught on a pole in the Jordan River near 365 North Twefth West, Salt Lake City; location on map is approximate. The baby had been in the water an estimated three months.

people.name: Baby Jane Doe
people.narrative:
Found September 24, 1943, in a trash can on the grounds of the City and County Building at 451 Washington Square, Salt Lake City.
Two boys were looking for soda bottles found the body of a newborn baby girl inside a paper sack, wrapped in the September 12th edition of a local newspaper.
The city physician/police surgeon performed an autopsy on the body and found that the baby died of exposure. Additionally, it was suggested the baby lived at least 24 hours and had been dead about 72 hours before it was found.
The baby girl was described as white/Caucasian and weighted about nine pounds.
She was never identified. Baby Jane Doe was buried on September 30th, 1943, in Salt Lake City Cemetery.

people.name: Baby Jane Doe
people.narrative:
Found July 11, 1948, floating in the Bear River by fishermen about 350 yards south and east of the old bridge at Corrine.
The body badly decomposed and an autopsy could not be performed to find a cause of death. Some discrepancy to whether the baby was male or female (though this could just be error). It was theorized the body was in the river a week to more than 10 days.
The baby was never identified. She was buried July 12, 1948, in the Brigham City Cemetery.

Box Elder County Sheriff's Office Case #: 48-00001

people.name: Baby Jane Doe
people.narrative:
Found October 21, 1950, by a couple in a virtually dry Provo riverbed, 3 miles from the mouth of Provo Canyon, not far from Christmas City Cafe/Tavern.
The body was wrapped in a sheet and weighed down with a large rock while the water was probably still running. Water was turned out of the river about two days before at Murdock diversion dam.
A Provo physician who inspected the body said the infant was at least a seven-month fetus, but he was unable to determine whether or not an abortion was involved. The death certificate described the baby as a near term infant. The body was badly decomposed so neither a cause nor manner of death could be determined though her race was determined as white/Caucasian.
She was never identified and was buried in Provo City Cemetery on October 24, 1950.

Utah County Sheriff's Office Case #: 20UC11758

people.name: 1956/1957 Baby Jane Doe
people.narrative:
Found January 6, 1957, face down by a teenager in Big Cottonwood Creek at about 4100 S 3rd W, just outside of Murray city limits.
It was determined that the baby likely entered the icy creek while dead and could've been there for up to a month. Although decomposition made it difficult to determine the cause of death, authorities stated that there were no signs of trauma or violence to the body, and she likely died of hemorrhage or shock from a torn umbilical cord; or died of exposure.
She appeared to be less than 24 hours old, about two or three weeks premature, but would've likely lived with proper care.
Baby Jane Doe was never identified, and she was buried in the Salt Lake City Cemetery on January 10, 1957.

people.name: Baby Jane Doe
people.narrative:
Found April 26, 1961, in a pile of sawdust and refuse in dump pit #1 at 6400 W North Temple, Salt Lake City by a person looking for resalable items. Location on map is approximate.
The body had not been there more than two days and was likely left there the night before. The baby was full term and apparently did not have any wounds or evidence of violence, but the heavy equipment used for grading at the dump had mutilated the body.
She was taken to St. Marks Hospital for an autopsy, but it failed to find the cause of death. Baby Jane Doe was never identified, and she was buried in the Salt Lake City Cemetery on April 27, 1961.

people.name: Jane Doe
people.narrative:
Found October 1, 1970, buried about 5 feet from the county road to Gunlock by a crew operating a bulldozer as they were clearing a section of the roadside about 18 to 20 miles from St. George. Location on map is approximate.
An autopsy ruled the death was listed as undetermined. No clothing was found with her body. Dental work (filling) was noted.
The woman was described as white/Caucasian, 20 to 60 years old, about 5' 2" tall and weighed around 140 pounds. A few strands of fine light blond or gray hair were also found.
The Washington County Sheriff's Office had an order to exhume the decedent's body in 2014 in order to collect DNA. The grave site for Jane Doe was empty.

Washington County Sheriff’s Office Case #: 14W000613

people.name: Baby Jane Doe
people.narrative:
Found September 15, 1974,  floating about 70 yards from a 2inch flow pipe at the north end of Beus Pond near (1596 Country Hills Dr. Ogden) by three boys swimming. Officers located, separately, a plastic bag containing the placenta and umbilical cord.
The medical examiner determined the body had been dead and in the water probably 15-20 days. An autopsy could not determine a cause of death.
The baby was described as a white/Caucasian female, weighing between 6-8 pounds and was a full-term infant.
Baby Jane Doe was never identified, and she was buried in the Ogden City Cemetery on September 28th, 1974.

Ogden City Police Department Case #: 21G34090 OR 74-16576

people.name: Jane Doe
people.narrative:
Found May 1, 1982, on the top of a pile of river bottom material by a passerby walking along the Jordan River past a dredging site near 1600 N 1700 W, Salt Lake City. Location on map is approximate.
Police sifted the other dredged river bottom materials, but no other human bones were located.
The medical examiner believed the skull was very old. There was a bullet hole in the forehead with an exit in the back. The woman was described as white/Caucasian.

Salt Lake City Police Department Case #: 1982-35206

people.name: Jane Doe
people.narrative:
Found September 3, 1986, floating in a sewage canal east of the Salt Lake City water treatment plant near 1850 N 1300 W, Salt Lake City. Location on map is approximate.
The body was found dressed in a white blouse with five small, round white buttons down the front. The blouse is long sleeved with buttons on the cuffs. Both the cuffs and the collar have a lace design on them. The body was also wearing a pair of Levi denim pants with a button and zipper in them. The belt loops would only fit a thin belt. In the middle of the back of the pants, one belt loop criss-crosses. The pants have no pockets. The bra or underwear was located.
An 'Advance' brand wrist watch with a round, black face and an off-white checker pattern inside the glass was also found on the body. There are no numbers on the watch. Watch has a second hand. The bottom of watch it says 'Quartz' and 'Japan along with a design of a a swimmer. She also wore a dark metal ring with a black stone. The word 'Sterling' was stamped on the inner rim.
Near the body investigators found a pink plastic comb, a pink toothbrush, and a purple bracelet that unclicks into a pen. On the west canal bank, a blue and white dress was found. The dress appeared to be homemade, has a cardboard tag on it with the #10 written on it, but no other tags or markings.
She was described as white/Caucasian, 20 to 40 years old, 5' 6" tall and about 115 pounds. There was no head hair left, but she had black wavy pubic hair. It was estimated she had been dead for several months prior to discovery.

Salt Lake City Police Department Case #: 1986-87566

people.name: Jane Doe
people.narrative:
Found March 13, 2001, tied to a fence post by the sheriff's office while serving a drug search warrant on a 620 acre property about 5 miles northwest of Duchesne near Utahn. Location on map is approximate.
The remains were initially thought to be archaeological Native American, but recent forensic anthropological analysis determined the remains to be a modern Hispanic. Her race and ethnicity have been listed as mixed as well as Asian or Pacific Islander.
Jane Doe was described  as 17 tp 20 years old, and died 2 to 20 years before discovery.

Duchesne County Sheriff's Office Case #: 2020-000122

people.name: Frances Shurtleff Sessions
people.narrative:
Last seen May 20, 1946, at her parents home at 916 26th St, Ogden. Her son had just been killed in a rock slide in Weber Canyon minutes before, and there were some sightings of her walking toward the canyon.

Ogden City Police Department Case #: 22G14442

people.name: Sarah Luella "Ella" Snow
people.narrative:
Last seen September 13, 1953, leaving her family home in the vicinity of 10th S and 9th E, Springville. Her vehicle, a black sedan or coupe, was located near the rifle range at the mouth of Hobble Creek Canyon.

Springville Police Department Case #: 20SV06663

people.name: Ruby Mae Bosen
people.narrative:
Last seen about 3 p.m. on July 29, 1955, when a small, aluminum boat in which she, her husband, and her brother were riding overturned. She drowned and was never recovered.

Duchesne County Sheriff's Office Case #: M24-1508

people.name: Vilate Kerreen Young
people.narrative:
Last seen July 4, 1956, during a large family gathering at her grandfather's ranch, the Newel Steed Ranch, about 7 NW of Widtsoe.

Garfield County Sheriff's Office Case #: 0040786

people.name: Dennise Jeannette Sullivan
people.narrative:
Last seen July 4, 1961, not far from Dead Horse Point State Park Overlook area, Moab, when she was abducted by another motorist on the road after he shot her mother and mother's fiancée.

Grand County Sheriff's Office Case #: 18003841

people.name: Lana Lynn Lee
people.narrative:
Last seen September 17, 1961, attempting to cross Wahweap Wash south of Glen Canyon City. She was swept away in flash floodwaters.

Kane County Sheriff's Office Case #: S2203167

people.name: Jennifer "Jenny" Lee Klein
people.narrative:
Last seen May 25, 1974, playing in the sand along the Colorado River at a campground she was staying at with her family about 7 to 10 miles N of Moab near Big Bend at the Moose Park Campground. The adults were temporarily distracted and when they turned back around, Jenny was gone.

Grand County Sheriff's Office Case #: 2003000453

people.name: Nancy Wilcox
people.narrative:
Last seen October 1, 1974, leaving her home in the vicinity of 2409 Arnette Dr, Holladay. Ted Bundy confessed to her murder and claimed to have buried her body near Capitol Reef National Park.

Unified Police Department of Greater Salt Lake Case #: 74-54455

people.name: Susan Curtis
people.narrative:
Last seen June 27, 1975, near the Ernest L. Wilkinson Student Center at Brigham Young University in Provo while attending the Bountiful Orchard Youth Conference. Ted Bundy later confessed to her murder, and said he buried her body near Price.

BYU Police Department Case #: 14BY00782

people.name: Nancy Perry Baird
people.narrative:
Last seen July 4, 1975, while working as a clerk at the Fina Gas Station in the 200 block of South Highway 89, East Layton. She was last seen by a patrol officer 15 minutes before she was discovered missing. Her purse and money were found behind the counter. Her vehicle was left parker at the gas station.

Davis County Sheriff's Office Case #: D11-04588 OR 75-2873

people.name: Linda Sue Hanoian Peterson
people.narrative:
Last seen September 16, 1978, in Murray; exact location unknown/not released. Her husband, Mr. Peterson, said she stopped by his place of employment to tell him she was going to Kentucky. She was 5 months pregnant.

Murray Police Department Case #: 14C006557

people.name: Mary Katherine Miller
people.narrative:
Last seen June 20, 1979, at Price's Ice Cream Shop at 1270 N State St, Provo. She may have been going to American Fork Cemetery then up American Fork Canyon. Her vehicle, a 1978 brown Honda Civic, was found hidden near a sheep herders' camp beyond the Timpooneke campground on the back side of Mount Timpanogos with her keys, purse, and identification inside.

Utah County Sheriff's Office Case #: 793172

people.name: Sydnee Lee Porter
people.narrative:
Last seen sometime in 1981 in Salt Lake City;  exact location unknown/not released. The only information is that she was last seen with Oscar Baker, and he is now deceased.

Unified Police Department of Greater Salt Lake Case #: CO-2018-117680

people.name: Debra "Debbie" Lee Frost
people.narrative:
Last seen July 9, 1984, at the Mountain Bell Plaza, 250 E 200 S, Salt Lake City. She may have been trying to walk or hitchhike to Montgomery St. About three weeks after she disappeared, she had contact with police near Rock Springs, Wyoming.

Salt Lake City Police Department Case #: 84-57524

people.name: Vickie Annette Smock
people.narrative:
Last seen in January 1985, when her father dropped her off at a Greyhound bus station in Salt Lake City;  exact location unknown/not released. It was believed she was going to Wendover, Nevada with an unknown male companion to work at the Peppermill Casino.

Salt Lake City Police Department Case #: 02-157398

people.name: Joyce Lynn Yost
people.narrative:
Last seen August 11, 1985, by friends at the Hill Air Force Base Officer's Club. Her vehicle was located abandoned one week later on a dirt road in Weber County, east of South Ogden. Douglas Lovell was convicted in 1985 of abducting and assaulting Joyce. He later pleaded guilty to her murder in an attempt to prevent her from testifying.

South Ogden Police Department Case #: 853955

people.name: Sheree L Warren
people.narrative:
Last seen October 2, 1985, leaving the parking garage behind her place of employment at the Utah State Employees' Credit Union headquarters at 660 S 200 E, Salt Lake City. She said she was going to pick up her ex-husband at Wagstaff Toyota, a couple of blocks away. She lived with her parents and young son in Roy at the time. Her vehicle, a 1984 Toyota Corolla with Utah plate # VSW649, was later found abandoned in the parking lot of the Aladdin Hotel in Las Vegas, Nevada.

Roy Police Department Case #: 856792

people.name: Marlene Ann Puchuskie
people.narrative:
Last seen June 24, 1986, in Salt Lake City; exact location unknown/not released.

Unified Police Department of Greater Salt Lake Case #: CO1986-54420

people.name: Rhonda Fae Karren
people.narrative:
Last seen September 28, 1987, at her home in Vernal; exact address unknown/not released. She had attended a work seminar in Salt Lake City that evening. Her purse, keys, and notes from the seminar were on the table inside her residence. Her vehicle was left in the driveway, but there were signs of a struggle in the yard (none inside the residence).

Uintah County Sheriff's Office Case #: 8700758

people.name: Annette A Hill
people.narrative:
Last seen May 12, 1989, by her daughter leaving their apartment in the vicinity of 8200 South and 1220 East, Sandy. Thomas Trujillo confessed to murdering her while she used a payphone at 900 E S Temple St, Salt Lake City and put her body in a dumpster nearby.

Sandy Police Department Case #: 893415

people.name: Sarah Ann Norfleet
people.narrative:
Last seen June 20, 1993, when she was dropped off near her family home at 512 E 200 N, Bountiful, by her friend and their parents. She may have traveled to Nevada or California with a male companion.

Bountiful Police Department Case #: 9314195

people.name: Bobbi Ann Campbell
people.narrative:
Last seen around December 30, 1994, leaving her home in the vicinity of E 11900 S, Draper. She planned go pick up her paycheck from SOS Staffing Services, go to the bank, and then the grocery store. Nine months after she disappeared, her vehicle was found abandoned at 200 South 1100 W near the Jordan River. Rumors circulated that she died of a drug overdose and her body was dumped in the Jordan river near Alzheimer's Park.

Unified Police Department of Greater Salt Lake Case #: 95-2784

people.name: Kiplyn Davis
people.narrative:
Last seen May 2, 1995, at lunchtime at Spanish Fork High School at 99 N 300 W, Spanish Fork. In 2011, Timmy Brent Olsen pleaded guilty to manslaughter. It is believed she was killed and buried in Spanish Fork Canyon, but Olsen has refused to help locate her body.

Spanish Fork Police Department Case #: 950810

people.name: Rosa Maria Shipley
people.narrative:
Last seen sometime in 1998 outside a women's shelter in Salt Lake City. She left her home in Seguin, Texas after Mother's Day in May of 1997. While she has a history of being homeless and an undiagnosed mental illness, she had always kept in touch with her family.

Seguin (TX) Police Department Case #: 1700025351
Salt Lake City Police Department Case #: 1700025351

people.name: Ruth Jimenez
people.narrative:
Last seen July 1, 1999,  in Sandy; exact location unknown/not released. She disappeared under suspicious circumstances.

Sandy Police Department Case #: 00-1661

people.name: Janis "Jan" Marie Stavros
people.narrative:
Last seen January 3, 2001, at her home at 2910 E. 3215 South, Millcreek. Her vehicle was still at her home. None of her personal items were missing, but investigators found blood and blood-stained items inside her vehicle and residence.

Unified Police Department of Greater Salt Lake Case #: CO2001-1275

people.name: Aletha "Ally" Jo Williams
people.narrative:
Last seen March 6, 2001, in the vicinity of 1700 South and West Temple, Salt Lake City, after dropping some things off at a relative's house. She was about 6 months pregnant at the time. Authorities named Michael Vincent as her killer, but her body has never been located.

Salt Lake City Police Department Case #: 02-79626

people.name: Stephanie Michele Smith
